question: A product page uses very light grey text on a white background. What is the main issue?
choices: The text contrast is too low to read easily
The font weight is too thin for body text
The text size is too small for this layout
explanation: This affects many users, especially people with low vision, because the text becomes hard to read. The safer fix is to increase the contrast so the content remains readable. Think: who is blocked when text fades into the background?
learning_objective: Prioritize the main readability issue when contrast, size, and weight all seem relevant.

question: A checkout page shows an important image with alt="image.png". What is the main issue?
choices: The alt text does not explain the meaning of the image
The image should also include a visible caption
The file name should match the product title
explanation: This affects screen reader users because the image meaning is lost or unclear. The better choice is to replace the file name with alt text that explains the image purpose. Ask yourself: what happens if the image cannot be seen?
learning_objective: Distinguish between naming an image and explaining its meaning.

question: A sign-up form has visible field names, but the inputs are not linked to labels in the code. What is the main issue?
choices: The fields need clearer visual grouping
The fields do not have clear labels for assistive tools
The form asks for too much information at once
explanation: This affects screen reader and speech input users because the field purpose may not be announced correctly. The right fix is to connect each visible label to its input in the code. Think: how would someone know what to enter here?
learning_objective: Decide between a visual form issue and a code-level labeling issue.

question: A page uses large bold text for section titles, but not real heading elements. What is the main issue?
choices: The page structure is unclear to assistive tools
The sections look too visually dominant
The headings need more spacing above them
explanation: This affects users who navigate by structure because styled text is not the same as a real heading. The better solution is to use heading elements so the page outline is clear in the code. What user would struggle if structure is only visual?
learning_objective: Distinguish between visual hierarchy and structural hierarchy.

question: A short explainer video includes speech but no captions. What is the main issue?
choices: The video needs a transcript on a separate page
Users cannot get the spoken information in text form
The narration should be slowed down for clarity
explanation: This affects deaf and hard-of-hearing users because spoken information is missing in the video itself. The better approach is to provide captions so the audio meaning is available at the same moment. Think: who loses the message when sound is the only channel?
learning_objective: Prioritize the best way to make spoken video content available.

question: A menu opens only when a user clicks it with a mouse. What is the main issue?
choices: The menu is not usable without a mouse
The menu state is not visible enough after it opens
The click target is too small for quick use
explanation: This affects keyboard-only users because they may not be able to open the menu at all. The main fix is to make the same menu action work from the keyboard. Ask yourself: can the task start without a mouse?
learning_objective: Distinguish between discoverability issues and access-to-function issues.

question: A form shows errors only by turning fields red. What is the main issue?
choices: The error state depends only on color to communicate meaning
The contrast of the red may be too weak against the background
The form should place all error messages at the top of the page
explanation: This affects users who do not perceive color differences clearly because the error meaning may disappear. The better choice is to add text or another clear cue, not only color. Think: would the error still make sense in grayscale?
learning_objective: Decide when color is the problem and when contrast is the problem.

question: A modal opens, but keyboard focus stays on the page behind it. What is the main issue?
choices: The background content should be dimmed more strongly
The keyboard focus is left in the wrong place
The modal needs a clearer title so users notice it faster
explanation: This affects keyboard and screen reader users because they may keep interacting with the wrong part of the page. The right fix is to move focus into the modal when it opens. Ask yourself: where does the next keyboard action go?
learning_objective: Prioritize focus behavior over purely visual modal design issues.

question: A service logs users out after 30 seconds with no warning. What is the main issue?
choices: The timeout reason is not explained clearly enough
Users are not given enough time or control
The logout should happen after a confirmation step
explanation: This affects users who need more time to read, think, or type because the task may end too early. The better design is to warn users and let them extend the session. Think: who is pressured when the system controls the pace?
learning_objective: Distinguish between explaining a timeout and giving users control over it.

question: A custom button is announced as a button, but pressing Enter or Space does nothing. What is the main issue?
choices: The control should look more like a standard button
The control has a role but not the expected behavior
The control needs more helper text to explain what it does
explanation: This affects keyboard and assistive technology users because the control promises button behavior but does not deliver it. The correct fix is to make the custom control behave like a real button. Ask yourself: does the control act the way it is announced?
learning_objective: Distinguish between a visual consistency issue and a behavior mismatch.

question: An automated hiring tool rejects applicants, gives no reason, and offers no human review path. What is the main accessibility issue?
choices: The decision flow is not transparent and gives users no fair way to challenge an outcome
The tool should explain the scoring model in more technical detail
The system should ask fewer questions before making a decision
The main problem is that the dashboard is too hard to scan visually
explanation: This affects people blocked by an important automated decision because they cannot understand or challenge the result. The better design is to provide a clear reason and a human review path. Think: who is excluded when there is no way to appeal?
learning_objective: Prioritize fairness and recourse over interface polish in automated systems.

question: A booking form works only in portrait mode on a phone. What is the main issue?
choices: The layout blocks people who need another screen orientation
The mobile form should show fewer fields at once
The page should adapt more smoothly at different screen widths
The landscape version should simplify the visual layout
explanation: This affects users who rely on a different device position because they may not be able to use the form at all. The better fix is to support both orientations unless one is truly essential. Ask yourself: what happens if the device cannot be held that way?
learning_objective: Distinguish between orientation restriction and general responsive design improvement.

question: A mobile app uses very small action buttons placed close together. What is the main issue?
choices: The icon style makes each action harder to recognize quickly
The targets are too small and too close to tap reliably
The action labels should be shortened before the buttons are resized
The spacing problem matters only on the smallest screen sizes
explanation: This affects users with limited precision because they may hit the wrong action or miss it entirely. The better design is to enlarge the targets and separate them more clearly. Think: who would struggle to tap this accurately?
learning_objective: Prioritize reliable input over general visual cleanup.

question: A help page uses dense legal wording with no simple explanation. What is the main issue?
choices: The writing is hard to understand for many users
The page should shorten the paragraphs before changing the wording
The title should summarize the policy more clearly
The tone is too formal for the rest of the product
explanation: This affects users with different reading and language needs because the content may be hard to understand or act on. The better choice is to rewrite key instructions in plain language. Ask yourself: who understands this text on the first read?
learning_objective: Distinguish between tone, layout, and real comprehension barriers.

question: A user can permanently delete data with one tap and no confirmation or undo. What is the main issue?
choices: The delete action is too prominent compared with nearby actions
The action is too easy to trigger by mistake with no recovery path
The destructive action should appear only in account settings
The warning color is not strong enough for a risky action
explanation: This affects all users, especially people who act quickly or by mistake, because the loss cannot be recovered. The better design is to add confirmation, undo, or another recovery option. Think: what happens after one accidental tap?
learning_objective: Prioritize recoverability over visual treatment of risky actions.

question: A video starts with sound as soon as the page loads. What is the main issue?
choices: The page feels too busy because several things compete for attention
Users lose control over audio and may struggle to focus on the page
The player should show captions immediately when autoplay begins
The audio should start lower in volume rather than at the default level
explanation: This affects users who need quiet control of the interface because unexpected sound can interrupt reading and assistive technology use. The better solution is to let users choose when audio starts. Ask yourself: who loses focus when sound begins without warning?
learning_objective: Decide between attention problems and loss of user control over sound.

question: A search button shows only a magnifying glass icon and has no accessible name in the code. What is the main issue?
choices: The icon may be understood visually, but the control still lacks a clear name for assistive tools
The icon is too abstract and should always be replaced by visible text
The control should include helper text below it instead of next to it
The search button should be visually larger before its label is changed
explanation: This affects screen reader and speech users because the control may not be identified clearly. The better fix is to keep or improve the icon if needed, but also add a proper accessible name. Think: how would this control be announced out loud?
learning_objective: Distinguish between visible clarity and programmatic naming.

question: A dashboard breaks and hides content when the page is zoomed in. What is the main issue?
choices: The dashboard should show fewer cards once the screen gets crowded
The layout does not adapt when users zoom and enlarge content
The page should switch breakpoints sooner for medium-width screens
The browser zoom setting should be limited to avoid layout problems
explanation: This affects users who zoom to read because important content or actions may disappear. The better approach is to let the layout reflow instead of breaking under zoom. Ask yourself: what becomes unreachable when content grows larger?
learning_objective: Prioritize preserved access under zoom over general layout optimization.

question: A page has several links that all say "Click here". What is the main issue?
choices: The wording is too informal for a professional service
The links do not explain their destination or action clearly enough
The links should include icons so users can compare them more quickly
The repeated wording is acceptable if the links are visually separated
explanation: This affects users who scan links out of context because they cannot tell where each one goes. The better design is to make each link text explain its destination or action. Think: what would a user know before activating the link?
learning_objective: Distinguish between visual grouping and meaningful link text.

question: A public service can only be accessed through a mobile app with biometric login. What is the main issue?
choices: The service gives users only one access path and no practical alternative
The biometric process should be explained more clearly before login starts
The app should offer another identity check inside the same flow
The service should prefer web access because apps are harder to maintain
explanation: This affects people who cannot or do not want to use that one method because the service becomes unreachable. The better design is to offer another practical way to access the service. Ask yourself: who is excluded by this single path?
learning_objective: Prioritize alternative access paths over improvements to one restricted path.
